1.description: Career in finance and household stock market participation. This figure summarizes stock market participation rates among households in which neither spouse works in finance, those in which only the wife works in finance, those in which only the husband works in finance, and those in which both spouses work in finance. A household participates in the stock market if any family member holds any stock or mutual fund. Data are from the Annual Social and Economic Supplement of the Current Population Survey for 1988–2018 and the sample is limited to nonmilitary married couples aged between 24 and 64 who do not live in group quarters. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Experimental design. This figure summarizes the design of the randomized controlled experiment. Subjects are recruited from Amazon's Mechanical Turk and the sample is restricted to married individuals aged between 24 and 64 who are U.S. residents. They are first introduced to an employee stock purchase plan and are asked whether they plan to participate. All subjects then complete a writing task in which some of them are randomly assigned to the primed condition where gender identity is made salient to them, whereas the rest are assigned to the control condition. Finally, subjects are asked to make the participation decision again in a designed scenario in which their spouse has an opposing viewpoint. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Causal impacts of gender identity. This figure presents univariate evidence from a randomized controlled experiment designed to study the causal impacts of gender identity on intrahousehold financial decision-making. Panel A plots, by randomization condition, women's influence at the information contribution stage of the financial decision-making process between spouses. The sample is restricted to female subjects who chose to participate in the employee stock purchase plan. The subject is willing to contribute ideas to her husband if she contradicts her husband, who is an eligible employee but not inclined to enroll in the plan. Panel B plots, by randomization condition, men's influence at the information aggregation stage of the financial decision-making process between spouses. The sample is restricted to male subjects who did not choose to enroll in the plan. The subject is open to advice from his wife if he chooses to participate in the plan after hearing the advice from his wife, who by design has the correct reasoning. In both panels, error bars indicate standard errors of the arithmetic mean. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
